differential.evidence_basis: Intense nocturnal scalp itch with a crawling/tickling sensation in a school-aged child in a shared-sleeping household is the classic presentation of head lice.
Scalp itch and white flakes can mimic infestation; distinguished by loose, easily-removed flakes rather than firmly adherent nits, and absence of live movement.
Itchy scalp in a child can be tinea; would expect scaly patches of hair loss with broken 'black-dot' hairs or a boggy kerion, none reported here.

differential.diagnosis: Atopic or contact dermatitis of the scalp
differential.evidence_basis: Can cause scalp pruritus; does not explain the crawling sensation or adherent specks, though atopy can amplify the itch reaction.

differential_progression.scoring_note: On the opening complaint alone, head lice leads but the AI Doctor should keep dandruff and tinea capitis on the list until the discriminating features are elicited.

differential.evidence_basis: Firmly adherent greyish-white specks close to the scalp behind the ears and occiput plus caregiver-observed movement and failure of anti-dandruff shampoo are highly specific for active infestation.
Adherent nits that do not flick off, occipital/retroauricular clustering, and visible movement are not features of dandruff; non-response to anti-dandruff shampoo further argues against it.
Still worth keeping in mind if scaly bald patches or a kerion later appear, but no alopecia or broken 'black-dot' hairs are described.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The adherent-nit-vs-flaking-dandruff distinction and caregiver-reported movement are the decisive history features. This is a straightforward case once elicited.

differential.diagnosis: Pediculosis capitis (active head lice infestation), uncomplicated, with excoriation
differential.evidence_basis: Active infestation confirmed by adherent nits within 1 cm of the scalp and visualised lice; excoriated but no current secondary bacterial infection. Benign and telehealth/pharmacist-manageable.

differential.diagnosis: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated scalp (impetiginisation/cellulitis)
differential.evidence_basis: The one complication to safety-net for: currently absent (no honey-coloured crusting, pus, spreading erythema, or fever) but the raw excoriations are a portal of entry — flag the warning signs.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct endpoint is home treatment plus a safety-net for secondary bacterial infection. Escalating this benign infestation to urgent in-person care or 000 is over-triage and the primary failure mode of the case.

red_flags.description: Signs of secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated scalp/neck — thick yellow (honey-coloured) crusting, pus, or hot, swollen, spreading redness (impetigo/cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The scratched, broken skin is a portal for Staphylococcus/Streptococcus. If it becomes infected the child needs prompt assessment and topical or oral antibiotics — this is the escalation trigger for an otherwise benign condition.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated impetiginisation can progress to cellulitis or, rarely, systemic infection; missing it turns a trivial problem into one needing urgent care.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Mild tender occipital/posterior cervical nodes are a common reactive finding with an inflamed, excoriated scalp. If nodes become large, hot, very painful or fluctuant this suggests suppuration/abscess needing same-day review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing suppurative node or deeper infection could be missed if the reactive lymphadenopathy is not reassessed when the scalp infection worsens.

red_flags.description: Systemic features — high fever, lethargy, spreading erythema, or a generally unwell child
red_flags.clinical_significance: Head lice do not cause systemic illness. Fever or an unwell child points to a secondary bacterial infection or an unrelated illness and warrants urgent in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to safety-net for systemic features could delay recognition of significant secondary infection.

pathophysiology_summary: Pediculosis capitis is infestation of the scalp by the obligate human ectoparasite Pediculus humanus capitis. Adult lice feed on scalp blood; females cement eggs (nits) to hair shafts close to the scalp, and viable eggs are found within ~1 cm of the scalp base — the location of the specks here. Spread is chiefly by direct head-to-head contact and, less so, shared bedding/hats/brushes, explaining the household transmission concern. Pruritus is a delayed type IV hypersensitivity to louse saliva and can persist for days after the lice are killed. Repeated scratching causes excoriation, which is the main complication, providing a portal for secondary bacterial infection (impetigo/cellulitis) and reactive posterior cervical lymphadenopathy. The condition is benign and self-limiting with treatment; the clinical priorities are eradication (topical pediculicide plus mechanical nit removal, with retreatment to catch newly-hatched lice), household decontamination, symptom relief, and safety-netting for secondary infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swayed by the caregiver's distress, the child's sleeplessness, or the source note's urgent-home-visit/MBS framing into sending a benign, self-limiting infestation to urgent in-person care, ED, or 000. Head lice is telehealth/pharmacist-manageable — over-escalation is the main error to avoid.

Accepting a 'dandruff' framing and recommending more anti-dandruff shampoo. The discriminator is that nits are firmly adherent and do not flick off, cluster near the scalp behind the ears/occiput, and there is visible movement — none of which fit seborrhoeic dermatitis.

Recommending a pediculicide but omitting mechanical wet-combing/nit removal and the mandatory retreatment in 7-10 days. Pediculicides are less reliably ovicidal, so eggs surviving the first application re-establish the infestation without combing and a second treatment.
Failing to examine (via photo/video) or safety-net the excoriated skin for impetiginisation — honey-coloured crusting, pus, spreading redness, fever — which is the one thing that escalates this case.

Reaching for a sedating antihistamine (e.g. promethazine) to help the child sleep and inventing a paediatric dose. Promethazine carries respiratory-depression cautions in young children and no dose should be synthesised remotely; symptom relief should be conservative and dosing deferred to in-person/pharmacist review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct, magnified scalp examination or dermoscopy to definitively confirm live lice versus hair debris
Palpation of the posterior cervical lymph nodes (tender lymphadenopathy in the source was a clinician finding)
Microscopy of a plucked hair to confirm an intact louse or viable nit
Clinician-measured vital signs (the source's BP/HR/RR/SpO2 were measured on the home visit)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None emergent. This is telehealth/pharmacist-manageable: confirm as far as possible from caregiver photo/video and history, give home treatment, and safety-net. Escalate to same-day in-person review ONLY if secondary bacterial infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate confusion risk with seborrhoeic dermatitis if the caregiver cannot clearly visualise adherent nits or movement; mitigate by asking the caregiver to inspect behind the ears and the nape in good light, use a fine-tooth detection comb, or send a close-up photo.
